title: Ezogelin çorbası
subtitle: Red lentil and bulgur soup with mint butter
excerpt: A thick, warming soup of red lentils and bulgur, spiced with cumin, paprika, and dried mint, finished with a sizzle of butter and lemon at the table. One of Turkey's most beloved soups — made in fifteen minutes and eaten by the bowlful on cold days.
sourceNotes: Ezogelin çorbası is named after a folk figure from Gaziantep province in southeastern Turkey — a young bride (gelin) who made the soup, documented in Turkish oral tradition and regional cookbooks. The soup is associated with Gaziantep and the broader southeastern Anatolian region, where red lentils and bulgur wheat are dietary staples. It is documented in Turkish household cooking literature from the early twentieth century and is one of the most widely served soups in Turkey.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to 3 months. The soup thickens on freezing; add stock or water when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps refrigerated for 4 days. Reheats well.

content.text: Red lentils disintegrate as they cook, thickening the soup without any blending. The bulgur adds texture and body. Both are done in under 30 minutes from a standing start. The soup should be thick enough that a spoon dragged through it leaves a trail that fills slowly — if it is too thin, simmer uncovered for 5 more minutes.

The sizzling butter with dried mint and chilli is not optional. It goes on at the table, poured over each bowl, and transforms the soup from nourishing but plain into something that smells and tastes complete. A squeeze of lemon sharpens everything at the end.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 8 minutes until soft and pale gold. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Cook for 2 minutes, stirring, until fragrant. Add the rinsed {{red-lentils}} of lentils and the {{bulgur-wheat}} of bulgur. Pour in the {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Season with salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils have completely dissolved and the soup is thick.

Taste for salt. If the soup is thicker than you like, add a splash of hot water. Ladle into warm bowls. In a small pan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium-high heat until foaming. Add the {{mint-dried}} of dried mint and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes and swirl for 15 seconds. Pour sizzling over each bowl. Serve with {{lemon}} wedges to squeeze over.

items.symptom: Soup is thin and watery after 20 minutes
items.cause: Lentils not cooked long enough, or too much stock
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for another 5 to 10 minutes; the lentils should dissolve completely into the broth

items.symptom: Lentils scorched on the bottom of the pan
items.cause: Heat too high after adding the lentils; not stirred frequently enough
items.fix: Keep the heat at a gentle simmer; stir every few minutes, especially toward the end when the soup thickens

items.symptom: Soup has no depth of flavour despite good spices
items.cause: Spices added without toasting in oil first; or butter finish skipped
items.fix: Cook the spices in the oil with the onion for 2 full minutes before adding liquid; do not skip the mint butter

items.symptom: Bulgur texture is unpleasant and gluey
items.cause: Coarse bulgur used instead of fine; or too much bulgur relative to lentils
items.fix: Use fine bulgur for this soup; the ratio is 4:1 lentils to bulgur

content.text: Ezogelin çorbası is one of a small number of Turkish dishes that carry a personal name and a story. Ezo is a folk figure from the Gaziantep region of southeastern Turkey — a bride who made the soup for her new family and whose name attached itself to the preparation. The soup is among the most commonly served in Turkey, found in canteens, wedding kitchens, university cafeterias, and roadside restaurants, always alongside a wedge of lemon and sometimes a bread roll. Its popularity is a function of its accessibility: red lentils and bulgur are cheap, storable, and quick to cook, and the result is filling, flavourful, and comforting in a way that more elaborate preparations often are not.
